flashcards.front: What is the average weight of the normal adult right lung?
Which lung has three lobes and two fissures?
flashcards.back: The right lung [83].

flashcards.front: Why does aspirated foreign material tend to pass into the right lung?
flashcards.back: The right main bronchus is more vertical than the left [84].

flashcards.front: Define a 'Pulmonary Lobule'.
flashcards.back: A cluster of about 5 acini enclosed by visible fibrous septa [63].

flashcards.front: What part of the lung tissue is distal to a terminal bronchiole?
flashcards.back: The acinus [63].

flashcards.front: Which cells secrete surfactant in the lungs?
flashcards.back: Type II or granular pneumocytes [64, 65].

flashcards.front: What is the main function of surfactant?
flashcards.back: To lower surface tension and maintain alveolar stability [65].

flashcards.front: What are the 'Pores of Kohn'?
flashcards.back: Connections between adjacent alveoli that allow the passage of bacteria and exudate [85].

flashcards.front: Define 'Pneumatocele' in congenital lung disease.
flashcards.back: A single large congenital cyst occupying almost an entire lobe [2].

flashcards.front: Distinguish between Intralobar and Extralobar sequestration.
flashcards.back: Intralobar is within the pleural covering; Extralobar lies outside the pleural layer (often below the diaphragm) [4].

flashcards.front: What is the basic defect in neonatal ARDS (Hyaline Membrane Disease)?
flashcards.back: A deficiency of pulmonary surfactant, usually due to prematurity [82].

flashcards.front: Why do stillborn infants NOT develop Hyaline Membrane Disease?
flashcards.back: Entry of air into the alveoli is essential for hyaline membrane formation [82].

flashcards.front: Name three direct causes of Adult ARDS.
flashcards.back: Diffuse infections (viral pneumonia), oxygen toxicity, and aspiration of gastric contents [86].

flashcards.front: What are 'Stiff Lungs' in the context of ARDS?
flashcards.back: Lungs coated with hyaline membranes that lose surfactant and fail to respond to oxygen therapy [87, 88].

flashcards.front: What is the definition of Pulmonary Hypertension?
flashcards.back: A systolic blood pressure in the pulmonary arterial circulation above 30 mmHg [7].

flashcards.front: Who is the typical patient for Primary (Idiopathic) Pulmonary Hypertension?
flashcards.back: Young females between ages 20 and 40 [8].

flashcards.front: What are 'Plexiform lesions'?
flashcards.back: Intraluminal tufts of capillary formation in dilated arterial branches, seen in pulmonary hypertension [89].

flashcards.front: Define 'Consolidation' in pneumonia.
flashcards.back: The solidification of the lung due to acute inflammation of the parenchyma [12].

flashcards.front: Which organism causes 90% of all lobar pneumonias?
flashcards.back: Streptococcus pneumoniae (Pneumococcus) [90].

flashcards.front: What is 'Carnification' in pneumonia complications?
flashcards.back: Post-pneumonic fibrosis where the lung becomes tough and leathery [91].

flashcards.front: Define Bronchopneumonia.
flashcards.back: Infection of the terminal bronchioles that extends into surrounding alveoli resulting in patchy consolidation [92].

flashcards.front: What is the hallmark histological feature of Viral Pneumonia?
flashcards.back: Interstitial inflammation without alveolar exudate [23, 75].

flashcards.front: Which fungus is a common opportunistic infection in 100% of HIV/AIDS cases?
flashcards.back: Pneumocystis jirovecii [93].

flashcards.front: Which special stain is used to identify Pneumocystis jirovecii?
flashcards.back: Grocott’s methenamine-silver (GMS) stain [94].

flashcards.front: What is the clinical definition of Chronic Bronchitis?
flashcards.back: Persistent cough with expectoration for at least 3 months of the year for 2 consecutive years [24].

flashcards.front: What is the 'Reid Index'?
flashcards.back: The ratio of the thickness of submucosal glands to the total bronchial wall thickness [29].

flashcards.front: Define Pulmonary Emphysema.
flashcards.back: Permanent dilatation of air spaces distal to terminal bronchioles with destruction of their walls [31].

flashcards.front: Contrast 'Blue Bloaters' vs. 'Pink Puffers'.
flashcards.back: Blue Bloaters (Chronic Bronchitis) have cyanosis/edema; Pink Puffers (Emphysema) are well-oxygenated but have labored breathing [30, 95].

flashcards.front: Which emphysema type is linked to alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency?
flashcards.back: Panacinar (Panlobular) emphysema [96].

flashcards.front: What are 'Curschmann’s spirals'?
flashcards.back: Twisted strips of respiratory epithelium found in the mucus plugs of asthma patients [97].

flashcards.front: What are 'Charcot-Leyden crystals'?
flashcards.back: Diamond-shaped crystals derived from eosinophils found in the sputum of asthma patients [98].

flashcards.front: Define Bronchiectasis.
flashcards.back: Abnormal and irreversible dilatation of bronchi and bronchioles due to inflammatory weakening of walls [34].

flashcards.front: What is 'Kartagener’s syndrome'?
flashcards.back: A triad of bronchiectasis, situs inversus, and sinusitis due to immotile cilia [99].

flashcards.front: What is 'Honeycomb Lung'?
flashcards.back: End-stage lung with widespread destruction of alveolar capillary walls and cystic spaces, seen in ILDs [42].

flashcards.front: Define Pneumoconiosis.
flashcards.back: Lung disease caused by inhalation of dust, usually at work [43].

flashcards.front: What is 'Caplan’s syndrome'?
flashcards.back: The development of rheumatoid nodules in the lungs of workers with pneumoconiosis (CWP, silicosis, or asbestosis) [100].

flashcards.front: What is the pathognomonic feature of Silicosis?
flashcards.back: Silicotic nodules consisting of a hyalinised center with concentric collagen laminations [101].

flashcards.front: How do asbestos bodies appear microscopically?
flashcards.back: Beaded or dumbbell-shaped fibres coated with glycoprotein and haemosiderin [102, 103].

flashcards.front: List three cancers associated with asbestos exposure.
flashcards.back: Bronchogenic carcinoma, Malignant mesothelioma, and carcinomas of the esophagus/stomach/colon [104].

flashcards.front: What is Goodpasture’s Syndrome?
flashcards.back: Combination of necrotising haemorrhagic interstitial pneumonitis and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis [105].

flashcards.front: Which type of bronchogenic carcinoma is most common in women and non-smokers?
flashcards.back: Adenocarcinoma [106].

flashcards.front: What is 'Pancoast’s syndrome'?
flashcards.back: Apical lung cancer involving the brachial plexus and sympathetic chain, causing arm pain and Horner's syndrome [73].

flashcards.front: Name four peptide hormones produced ectopically by lung cancer.
flashcards.back: ACTH (Cushing's), ADH (hyponatremia), Parathormone (hypercalcemia), and Calcitonin [50].

flashcards.front: What is 'Status Asthmaticus'?
flashcards.back: A severe and unremitting form of asthma that may be fatal [33].

flashcards.front: What is the primary risk factor for Malignant Mesothelioma?
flashcards.back: Occupational exposure to asbestos (particularly crocidolite) [61].

flashcards.front: Define 'Chylothorax'.
flashcards.back: Accumulation of milky fluid of lymphatic origin in the pleural cavity, usually from thoracic duct rupture [54].

flashcards.front: What is a 'Tension Pneumothorax'?
flashcards.back: A flap-valve defect that allows air in during inspiration but prevents escape during expiration, causing circulatory failure [58].

flashcards.front: What are 'Smokers’ macrophages'?
flashcards.back: Intra-alveolar macrophages with abundant brown-black pigment seen in Desquamative Interstitial Pneumonia (DIP) [107].

flashcards.front: Which lung cancer type is known as 'Oat cell carcinoma'?
flashcards.back: Small cell carcinoma [108].

flashcards.front: What is 'Hamman-Rich syndrome'?
flashcards.back: A rapidly progressive form of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis causing death within 6 months [109].

flashcards.front: True or False: Asbestos exposure increases the risk of lung cancer more in smokers than non-smokers.
flashcards.back: True: Risk is 10x higher in smokers vs. 5x higher in non-smokers [78].
